title: Self Quiz Question Bank
description: Multiple-choice review questions aligned to the 24 lecture units.

questions.prompt: What does human-centered design put first?
questions.explanation: The lecture defines human-centered design as putting human needs, capabilities, and behavior first, then designing around them.

questions.prompt: Which set of fields was used in the lecture's 'three-legged stool' view of HCI?
options: Computer science, design, and behavioral sciences
questions.explanation: The slide presents HCI as combining computer science, design, and behavioral sciences.

questions.prompt: In the lecture's interaction sequence examples, an interactive system is characterized by repeated exchanges of what?
options: Input and output between human and system
questions.explanation: The slides use input and output loops to explain how people interact with systems over a sequence of actions.

questions.prompt: Which three elements appear in the ISO definition of usability discussed in class?
questions.explanation: Usability was framed around effectiveness, efficiency, and satisfaction for specified users doing specified tasks.

questions.prompt: Why does human-centered design matter in HCI?
options: Because systems should be designed around human needs and behavior
Because all users prefer complex interfaces
Because algorithms remove the need for design
Because interface design is only about graphics
questions.explanation: The usability lecture emphasizes starting with people rather than forcing people to adapt to the system.

questions.prompt: When analyzing usability, what should you pay attention to besides the interface itself?
options: The specified users and the tasks they are trying to complete
questions.explanation: Usability is contextual. The lecture stresses specified users and tasks, not abstract judgments detached from use.

questions.prompt: The gulf of execution is mainly about the difficulty of:
questions.explanation: Norman's gulf of execution concerns how easily users can figure out what actions to take to achieve their goals.

questions.prompt: What is a signifier in interface design?
options: A clue that makes an affordance known to users
A security warning shown after login
questions.explanation: The lecture distinguishes affordances from signifiers, where signifiers communicate what actions are possible.

questions.prompt: According to the lecture, good systems should be discoverable and provide understandable:
questions.explanation: Discoverability and feedback help users know what actions are available and whether those actions succeeded.

questions.prompt: What does PACT stand for?
questions.explanation: PACT is the framework introduced for thinking about human-centered systems in terms of people, activities, context, and technology.

questions.prompt: What is the primary purpose of the User Experience Questionnaire (UEQ) shown in class?
options: To measure user experience dimensions of a system
questions.explanation: The UEQ was presented as a way to evaluate user experience rather than raw technical performance.

questions.prompt: In PACT analysis, 'context' mainly refers to:
options: The setting and circumstances in which interaction happens
The age of the operating system
questions.explanation: Context includes the physical, social, and organizational circumstances around interaction.

questions.prompt: Which Gestalt law explains why nearby elements are often perceived as a group?
questions.explanation: The lecture uses Gestalt principles such as proximity to explain how visual structure communicates relationships.

questions.prompt: Why is visual hierarchy important in interface design?
options: It helps communicate importance and guide attention
It guarantees all users will like the design
It removes the need for content organization
questions.explanation: Visual hierarchy supports perception by helping users notice what matters first and interpret the layout more efficiently.

questions.prompt: What is a practical implication of limited human attention for interface design?
options: Too much clutter can hide important information
Users can reliably process everything on screen at once
questions.explanation: Because attention is limited, designers should reduce clutter and structure information deliberately.

questions.prompt: Which retrieval mechanism is generally easier for users than recall?
questions.explanation: The lecture explicitly contrasts recognition and recall, noting that recognition is easier for users.

questions.prompt: Working memory is best described as:
questions.explanation: The cognition lecture covers working memory as a short-term, limited-capacity part of human cognition.

questions.prompt: What is one role of rehearsal in memory?
options: It helps keep or strengthen information for later retrieval
It eliminates the need for interface cues
It increases the number of simultaneous goals a user can track without limit
It only matters for motor tasks, not cognition
questions.explanation: The lecture connects rehearsal to reducing forgetting and supporting retrieval from memory.

questions.prompt: According to Fitts' Law, movement time for pointing depends strongly on target distance and target:
questions.explanation: Fitts' Law models pointing time using target distance and width through the index of difficulty.

questions.prompt: Which target is generally faster to acquire under Fitts' Law?
options: A larger target that is closer
A smaller target that is farther away
questions.explanation: Larger and closer targets reduce index of difficulty and therefore reduce movement time.

questions.prompt: Steering Law is most relevant when the task involves:
options: Following a constrained trajectory such as a tunnel
questions.explanation: Steering Law extends pointing models to trajectory-based tasks such as moving through tunnels or paths.

questions.prompt: What does the power law of practice predict?
options: People get faster as they repeat a task
All menu structures become equally efficient
questions.explanation: The lecture describes the power law of practice as the pattern that repeated performance tends to become faster.

questions.prompt: The Keystroke-Level Model (KLM) is mainly used to:
options: Predict expert, error-free task execution time
Measure user emotions from facial expressions
questions.explanation: KLM is a predictive HCI model for estimating how long expert users take to perform tasks.

questions.prompt: What is computational UI design?
options: Using models and algorithms to evaluate or generate interface designs
Writing any code that happens to run in a browser
Converting sketches directly into finished products without iteration
questions.explanation: The lecture frames computational design as applying models and optimization to design problems such as menu layout.

questions.prompt: Which kind of data analysis primarily deals with numbers and trends?
questions.explanation: Quantitative analysis focuses on numerical data, measurements, and trends.

questions.prompt: An affinity diagram is most associated with which type of analysis?
questions.explanation: Affinity diagramming is a common qualitative analysis method for grouping and interpreting findings.

questions.prompt: What does triangulation mean in user research?
options: Using multiple data sources or perspectives to study the same issue
Running the same interview script three times
questions.explanation: The lecture defines triangulation as combining methods or perspectives to strengthen findings.

questions.prompt: A functional requirement describes:
options: What font family the app uses
questions.explanation: Functional requirements describe the product's actions or capabilities.

questions.prompt: A non-functional requirement is mainly about:
questions.explanation: Non-functional requirements describe qualities such as performance, usability, reliability, or accessibility.

questions.prompt: What is a mental model in HCI?
options: How a user thinks the system works
questions.explanation: The lecture distinguishes the user's mental model from the designer's and implementation models.

questions.prompt: What is a storyboard in HCI prototyping?
options: A series of sketches showing how a user progresses through a task
A list of backend API endpoints
A statistical summary of user study data
questions.explanation: The lecture introduces storyboards as sequential sketches used to communicate task flow and experience.

questions.prompt: A horizontal prototype is mainly used to explore:
options: Deep functionality of one feature only
questions.explanation: Horizontal prototypes cover wide surface area, while vertical prototypes go deep into a narrower slice.

questions.prompt: Why are paper prototypes useful early in design?
options: They let teams simulate interactions quickly and cheaply
They remove the need for evaluation
questions.explanation: Paper prototyping is valuable because it is fast, low-cost, and easy to revise during early design.

questions.prompt: What is a Wizard of Oz prototype?
options: A prototype in which a hidden human simulates system intelligence or behavior
A finished product released to the public
A prototype built only with physical materials
A prototype generated entirely by telemetry logs
questions.explanation: Wizard of Oz prototyping uses a hidden person to mimic behavior that the future system may automate.

questions.prompt: Physical prototyping is especially useful when:
options: The product's form and feel matter to the interaction
Only spreadsheet formulas are being tested
The system already has a complete codebase
questions.explanation: The lecture points to physical prototypes when shape, feel, or embodied interaction matters.

questions.prompt: Which statement best matches the lecture's discussion of fidelity?
options: Low fidelity is common earlier; medium and high fidelity are more common later
High fidelity should always be used before any user feedback
Fidelity only matters for physical products
Low fidelity prototypes are more expensive than high fidelity ones
questions.explanation: The course frames fidelity as a design-stage choice, with lower fidelity common earlier and higher fidelity later.

questions.prompt: In a controlled experiment, the independent variable is the factor that is:
options: Always equal to the sample size
questions.explanation: Independent variables are the conditions or factors that researchers change to observe their effects.

questions.prompt: What defines a between-subjects design?
options: Each participant experiences only one study condition
Participants are tested in pairs only
questions.explanation: The lecture contrasts between-subjects with designs in which participants encounter multiple conditions.

questions.prompt: Which statistical test from the lecture is appropriate for comparing the means of two independent samples?
questions.explanation: The t-test lecture material specifically covers comparing two independent samples.

questions.prompt: Why are expert-based evaluation methods often used?
options: They can be faster and cheaper than user evaluations
They always replace user testing entirely
They only focus on visual style
questions.explanation: The lecture highlights expert methods as relatively fast and cheap, while also noting they do not replace user evaluation.

questions.prompt: A cognitive walkthrough mainly evaluates:
options: How easy it is for users to learn and complete an action sequence
Whether the code passes unit tests
How many users can fit in a room
How quickly a database query runs
questions.explanation: Cognitive walkthroughs step through tasks and ask whether users will know what to do at each step.

questions.prompt: Heuristic evaluation is based on:
options: Rules of thumb such as Nielsen's usability heuristics
questions.explanation: The lecture describes heuristic evaluation as an inspection method grounded in usability heuristics.

questions.prompt: Which statement best captures the course definition of accessibility?
options: Removing barriers that would otherwise exclude some people from using the system
questions.explanation: The lecture frames accessibility as removing barriers that can otherwise exclude people from use.

questions.prompt: What is the curb cut effect?
options: An accessibility improvement designed for one group also benefits many others
A method for cutting down app loading time
questions.explanation: Curb cuts help wheelchair users, but the lecture uses them to show how inclusive design often benefits broader populations.

questions.prompt: What does it mean to say disability or impairment can be situational?
options: Context can temporarily create accessibility barriers for many users
Disability is always permanent and identical across users
Accessible design is only relevant in specialized products
questions.explanation: The lecture emphasizes that context can create temporary or situational limitations, broadening why accessibility matters.

questions.prompt: How did the lecture classify groupware systems?
questions.explanation: Groupware was organized using the classic time-space matrix.

questions.prompt: Which example fits the 'different place, same time' category of groupware?
options: A shared whiteboard in one physical room
A solo spreadsheet on a laptop
questions.explanation: Different place, same time tools support synchronous collaboration across distance.

questions.prompt: What is crowdsourcing?
options: Taking work traditionally done by specific agents and opening it to a large crowd
Adding more tabs to a browser interface
A method for compressing video streams
Running expert heuristic evaluations in parallel
questions.explanation: The lecture defines crowdsourcing as opening work to a broad crowd through platforms and distributed participation.

questions.prompt: What is mixed-initiative design?
options: Designing interfaces where humans and intelligent agents collaborate
Showing two interfaces side by side for comparison
questions.explanation: The lecture presents mixed-initiative interfaces as systems where users and agents both contribute to the interaction.

questions.prompt: Direct manipulation is most closely associated with which interaction tradition?
questions.explanation: Direct manipulation is a core idea in GUI design and was contrasted with interface agents in the lecture.

questions.prompt: When should a system favor augmentation over full automation?
options: When the user should remain involved and AI uncertainty or cost of error matters
When the system has no interface
When only file storage is needed
When the goal is to remove all user choice regardless of consequences
questions.explanation: The lecture asks designers to decide when AI should assist users versus act autonomously, especially when uncertainty and stakes are high.

questions.prompt: Which of the following was listed as a challenge in designing AI-powered systems?
options: Too many standardized accessibility rules for AI
questions.explanation: The lecture names lack of AI knowledge, prototyping difficulty, and communicating impact or errors as design challenges.

questions.prompt: One Microsoft-inspired guideline discussed in class is to initially:
options: Force automation before explaining the system
questions.explanation: A core early-stage AI guideline is to set expectations so users are not surprised by system behavior.

questions.prompt: Which characteristic of human-AI collaboration focuses on leveraging the strengths of both sides?
questions.explanation: Complementary performance means combining the strengths of humans and AI to outperform either alone.

questions.prompt: What does WIMP stand for?
questions.explanation: The lecture contrasts classic WIMP interfaces with later post-WIMP interaction styles.

questions.prompt: Post-WIMP interfaces are characterized by:
options: Interaction modalities beyond classic GUI widgets
The removal of all input devices
questions.explanation: Post-WIMP interfaces extend beyond windows, icons, menus, and pointers into modalities such as touch, speech, and body input.

questions.prompt: A key goal of virtual reality is to create:
options: A strong illusion of being in or acting within a virtual world
An interaction with no sensory output
questions.explanation: The lecture frames VR around producing convincing illusions of presence and interaction in virtual environments.

questions.prompt: In VR, locomotion refers to:
options: How users move through virtual space
How sketches are turned into code
questions.explanation: The lecture defines locomotion as movement in VR and surveys techniques such as room-scale movement, joystick movement, and teleportation.

questions.prompt: What are 'beyond-real' or 'magical' interactions in VR?
options: Interactions that give users superhuman abilities to make tasks easier
Interactions that always mimic the physical world exactly
Interactions that remove the need for user input
questions.explanation: The lecture contrasts reality-based interaction with magical interaction that intentionally exceeds real-world constraints.

questions.prompt: Which technique is commonly used for selecting far-away objects in VR?
questions.explanation: Ray casting is a standard distant-selection technique discussed in the VR interaction lecture.

questions.prompt: Which of the following was presented as a key XR accessibility area?
options: Reducing all interfaces to text terminals
questions.explanation: The lecture highlights content accessibility, accessible input, and inclusive representations as key XR accessibility areas.

questions.prompt: One challenge of touchscreens discussed in class is that they can increase the:
questions.explanation: Without tactile cues, users may have more difficulty translating intentions into confident actions on touchscreens.

questions.prompt: Gesture-based interfaces primarily rely on:
options: Sensing and interpreting hand or body movement
Only mouse movement on a desktop
questions.explanation: The lecture covers gesture input as a category of hand and body based interaction that depends on movement sensing.

questions.prompt: In a directed-dialog speech interface, who mainly controls the conversation flow?
questions.explanation: Directed-dialog interfaces were described as systems that guide and constrain the structure of the conversation.

questions.prompt: Which was listed as a major advantage of speech-based interaction?
options: People can usually speak faster than they type
questions.explanation: The lecture notes that speech input can be fast, while also discussing tradeoffs such as sequential interaction and recognition issues.

questions.prompt: Why is gaze often combined with another input modality?
options: Because gaze alone can be error-prone and may trigger unintended selections
Because gaze only works on paper
Because gaze has no relationship to selection tasks
questions.explanation: The lecture discusses combining gaze with other inputs to reduce errors and avoid unintended activation.

questions.prompt: How was haptics characterized in the lecture?
options: As an interdisciplinary field spanning human, machine, computer, and interaction haptics
As a purely visual design method
As a kind of database indexing
As a replacement for all other modalities
questions.explanation: The lecture explicitly presents haptics as an interdisciplinary field with multiple subareas.

questions.prompt: What is tactile apparent motion illusion?
options: A perceived sense of continuous tactile movement created from separate actuations
A screen animation that looks like touch
A way to make objects visually disappear
questions.explanation: The lecture explains that a sense of motion can be created through carefully timed stimulation from multiple actuators.

questions.prompt: C-tactile afferents are associated in the lecture with:
questions.explanation: The haptics material mentions C-tactile afferents in relation to affective and pleasant social touch.

questions.prompt: In multisensory interaction, haptic feedback can play what kind of role?
options: Complementary or reinforcing role alongside other modalities
Only decorative role with no perceptual value
A role unrelated to user performance or experience
A role limited to audio playback
questions.explanation: The lecture asks students to analyze when haptic feedback complements or reinforces information from other modalities.

questions.prompt: Which set of activities was used to describe the haptic interaction design process?
options: Browsing, sketching, refining, and sharing or evaluating
questions.explanation: The lecture introduces four activities for haptic design: browsing, sketching, refining, and sharing or evaluating.

questions.prompt: What is Haptipedia used for in the haptics design process?
options: Browsing and exploring haptic examples or design ideas
questions.explanation: Haptipedia is presented as a resource for browsing haptic effects and inspiration during design.
